content.text: Mock Audits, Findings and Remediation Tracking

A mock audit is a structured simulation of an external or internal audit, conducted by personnel who are independent of the controls being tested. Its primary output is a written list of findings — gaps in evidence, design weaknesses, or operational failures — that the organisation has enough time to fix before the real audit.

The finding remediation lifecycle — classify, assign, fix, verify, close — mirrors the exception management process used for real audit findings. Using the same schema for both ensures that mock audit outputs translate directly into the organisation's risk management workflow and are not treated as second-class exercises.
A well-run mock audit covers the same scope as the real audit but is time-boxed. The recommended structure for a SOC 2 / MRM combined mock audit of Claude controls:
Control owner presents evidence package for 2–3 selected controls
Deep technical questions on logging architecture, test coverage, model versioning
Questions on consent management, DSR handling, incident response
Questions on human oversight, override process, model performance
Mock auditor presents preliminary findings; owners respond immediately
An internal auditor from a different business unit, or a senior compliance professional who did not design the controls being tested. Cost-effective; limited independence signal.

A qualified auditor or compliance consultant hired specifically for the mock audit. Higher cost but provides a more realistic simulation of real audit scrutiny and may surface issues an internal reviewer would miss.
Some firms in the same industry (non-competing) exchange mock auditors. Provides genuine independence and sector-specific knowledge without the full cost of a consultant.
The mock auditor must not have been involved in designing, implementing, or testing any of the controls they are reviewing. Even partial involvement compromises the independence signal.
If not remediated, the real auditor will issue a qualified opinion or material finding. Audit cannot proceed as planned.
Real auditor will likely raise a finding; could result in a management letter comment or condition on SOC 2 opinion.
Best-practice gap; real auditor may note it but unlikely to rise to a management letter finding.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from enum import Enum
from datetime import date, datetime, timezone
from pathlib import Path
import json

class FindingSeverity(str, Enum):
    BLOCKER = "blocker"
    SIGNIFICANT = "significant"
    OBSERVATION = "observation"

class FindingStatus(str, Enum):
    OPEN = "open"
    IN_PROGRESS = "in_progress"
    PENDING_VERIFICATION = "pending_verification"
    CLOSED = "closed"
    REOPENED = "reopened"

@dataclass
class MockAuditFinding:
    finding_id: str           # e.g. "MOCK-2026Q2-007"
    mock_audit_id: str        # links to the mock audit record
    control_id: str           # e.g. "CONS-001"
    raised_date: str          # ISO date
    raised_by: str
    title: str
    description: str
    severity: FindingSeverity
    status: FindingStatus
    assigned_to: str
    due_date: str             # ISO date
    remediation_plan: str = ""
    verification_notes: str = ""
    closed_date: str = ""
    reopen_reason: str = ""
    audit_trail: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)

    def log(self, action: str, actor: str) -> None:
        self.audit_trail.append({
            "ts_utc": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            "actor": actor,
            "action": action,
        })

    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        if self.status == FindingStatus.CLOSED:
            return False
        return date.today() > date.fromisoformat(self.due_date)

    def days_open(self) -> int:
        start = date.fromisoformat(self.raised_date)
        end = date.fromisoformat(self.closed_date) if self.closed_date else date.today()
        return (end - start).days

FINDINGS_LOG = Path("evidence/mock_audit/findings.jsonl")
FINDINGS_LOG.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

def save_finding(f: MockAuditFinding) -> None:
    record = asdict(f)
    FINDINGS_LOG.write_text(
        "\n".join(
            json.dumps(r) for r in
            ([json.loads(l) for l in FINDINGS_LOG.read_text().splitlines() if l]
             if FINDINGS_LOG.exists() else [])
            + [record]
        )
    )

# Example finding
finding = MockAuditFinding(
    finding_id="MOCK-2026Q2-001",
    mock_audit_id="MOCK-2026Q2",
    control_id="TOK-001",
    raised_date="2026-04-15",
    raised_by="External Mock Auditor, Jane Doe",
    title="T3 alert from March 12 has no corresponding exception ticket",
    description="API log INC-2026Q1-0312 shows a T3 alert (z-score 4.1) for user MDL-047 on 2026-03-12. No exception ticket found in the register for this event. The T3 alert should trigger an immediate incident declaration per the documented alert procedure.",
    severity=FindingSeverity.BLOCKER,
    status=FindingStatus.OPEN,
    assigned_to="Security Operations Lead",
    due_date="2026-05-01",
    remediation_plan="1. Investigate the March 12 alert to determine if an incident occurred. 2. Retroactively create exception ticket with full post-incident analysis. 3. Fix alert dispatch code to ensure T3 alerts always create a ticket automatically.",
)
finding.log("Finding raised in mock audit debrief", "Jane Doe (mock auditor)")
save_finding(finding)

Open findings must be tracked centrally so the compliance team and senior management can see the remediation status at any time. The dashboard feeds the pre-audit status report delivered to the audit committee.

from pathlib import Path
from datetime import date
import json

FINDINGS_LOG = Path("evidence/mock_audit/findings.jsonl")

def remediation_dashboard(mock_audit_id: str) -> dict:
    """Summarise open/closed findings for a given mock audit."""
    if not FINDINGS_LOG.exists():
        return {"error": "no findings log"}

    findings = [
        json.loads(l) for l in FINDINGS_LOG.read_text().splitlines() if l
    ]
    findings = [f for f in findings if f.get("mock_audit_id") == mock_audit_id]

    today = date.today().isoformat()
    by_status = {}
    overdue = []
    for f in findings:
        s = f.get("status", "unknown")
        by_status[s] = by_status.get(s, 0) + 1
        due = f.get("due_date", "")
        if due and due < today and s not in ("closed",):
            overdue.append(f["finding_id"])

    by_severity = {}
    for f in findings:
        sev = f.get("severity", "unknown")
        by_severity[sev] = by_severity.get(sev, 0) + 1

    total = len(findings)
    closed = by_status.get("closed", 0)
    return {
        "mock_audit_id": mock_audit_id,
        "total_findings": total,
        "closed": closed,
        "closure_rate_pct": round(closed / total * 100, 1) if total else 0,
        "overdue_finding_ids": overdue,
        "by_status": by_status,
        "by_severity": by_severity,
    }

report = remediation_dashboard("MOCK-2026Q2")
print(json.dumps(report, indent=2))
Common Mock Audit Findings for Claude Controls
Firms running their first mock audit of Claude controls frequently encounter these findings:
API call log missing for specific date in audit period
Log rotation deleted records before retention period; logging middleware restart dropped calls
Restore from backup; fix retention policy; add log completeness check to daily aggregator
System prompt changed without git commit
Developer edited prompt in production config directly; bypassed version control
Implement deploy pipeline gate that rejects configuration changes not traceable to a git commit
T3 alert has no corresponding exception ticket
Alert dispatch code has bug; database insert fails silently
Fix dispatch code; add dead-letter queue; add alert→ticket coverage test to CI
SOC 2 report for RAG platform vendor is 14 months old
TPRM compliance calendar not maintained; vendor not requested updated report
Update compliance calendar; request updated report immediately; add 60-day expiry alert
Validation report for Medium-tier model does not include sensitivity tests
Validator scoped the work narrowly; sensitivity tests considered optional
Add sensitivity tests as a mandatory section in the validation report template
Staff member with API access not in LMS training completion report
New hire completed onboarding training externally; not recorded in firm LMS
Sync external training records to LMS; add LMS check to offboarding/onboarding checklist

The majority of blocker findings in first-time mock audits relate to logging and evidence completeness, not control design. Controls that work operationally can still fail an audit if their evidence trail is incomplete.
A finding is not closed when the control owner says it is fixed. Closure requires independent verification by the compliance team — not the control owner — using the following steps:
Control owner notifies the compliance team that remediation is complete and provides a pointer to the new evidence (file path, git commit SHA, Jira ticket number).

Compliance team reviews the new evidence against the finding description. If the finding was 'T3 alert has no corresponding exception ticket', the evidence must show: (a) the retrospective ticket was created, and (b) a test confirming the dispatch code now creates tickets automatically.
If the evidence is sufficient, compliance team marks the finding CLOSED in the register and records the verification notes and the verifier's name.
If the evidence is insufficient, compliance team re-opens the finding with a note explaining what is still missing. The due date is not extended unless the compliance director approves.

After all blockers and significant findings are closed, the compliance team issues a mock audit close-out report to the audit committee summarising: total findings, closure rate, average days to close, and any findings carried forward as observations.

Many mock audit procedures can be automated, producing machine-verifiable evidence of control operation rather than relying solely on human walkthroughs. An automated mock audit suite runs the same checks a human auditor would perform, but faster and on the full population rather than a sample.

import hashlib, json
from datetime import date, timedelta
from pathlib import Path

# Directories referenced by previous sub-subtopics
EVIDENCE_ROOT   = Path("evidence")
DAILY_SUMMARIES = EVIDENCE_ROOT / "daily_summaries"
API_LOGS        = EVIDENCE_ROOT / "api_logs"
ALERT_LOG       = EVIDENCE_ROOT / "alerts" / "dispatch.jsonl"
EXCEPTION_LOG   = EVIDENCE_ROOT / "controls"
FINDINGS_OUT    = EVIDENCE_ROOT / "mock_audit" / "automated_findings.json"
FINDINGS_OUT.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

def run_automated_mock_audit(period_start: str, period_end: str) -> list[dict]:
    """
    Run automated checks against evidence collected during the audit period.
    Returns a list of finding dicts — empty means no issues found.
    """
    findings = []
    start = date.fromisoformat(period_start)
    end   = date.fromisoformat(period_end)

    # ── Check 1: Daily summary completeness ──────────────────────────────────
    current = start
    while current <= end:
        summary_file = DAILY_SUMMARIES / f"{current.isoformat()}.json"
        if not summary_file.exists():
            findings.append({
                "check": "daily_summary_completeness",
                "severity": "blocker",
                "date": current.isoformat(),
                "description": f"Missing daily summary for {current.isoformat()}",
            })
        current += timedelta(days=1)

    # ── Check 2: Daily summary integrity ─────────────────────────────────────
    for f in DAILY_SUMMARIES.glob("*.json"):
        record = json.loads(f.read_text())
        stored_sha = record.pop("sha256", None)
        if stored_sha:
            payload = json.dumps(record, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False).encode()
            actual = hashlib.sha256(payload).hexdigest()
            if actual != stored_sha:
                findings.append({
                    "check": "daily_summary_integrity",
                    "severity": "blocker",
                    "file": f.name,
                    "description": f"Integrity check failed for {f.name}",
                })

    # ── Check 3: T3 alerts have corresponding exception tickets ──────────────
    if ALERT_LOG.exists():
        for line in ALERT_LOG.read_text().splitlines():
            alert = json.loads(line)
            if alert.get("tier") == 3:
                alert_date = alert.get("ts", "")[:10]
                if alert_date < period_start or alert_date > period_end:
                    continue
                control_id = alert.get("control_id", "")
                # Look for a matching exception ticket
                ticket_found = any(
                    EXCEPTION_LOG.glob(f"{control_id}_*{alert_date.replace('-', '')}*.json")
                )
                if not ticket_found:
                    findings.append({
                        "check": "t3_alert_ticket_coverage",
                        "severity": "blocker",
                        "alert_ts": alert.get("ts"),
                        "control_id": control_id,
                        "description": f"T3 alert at {alert.get('ts')} has no exception ticket",
                    })

    # ── Check 4: Consent denial rate sanity (should be < 10%) ────────────────
    total_denied = 0
    total_calls = 0
    for f in DAILY_SUMMARIES.glob("*.json"):
        record = json.loads(f.read_text())
        if period_start <= record.get("date", "") <= period_end:
            cons = record.get("controls", {}).get("CONS-001", {})
            total_denied += cons.get("consent_denied", 0)
            total_calls  += record.get("total_calls", 0)

    if total_calls > 0:
        denial_rate = total_denied / total_calls
        if denial_rate > 0.10:
            findings.append({
                "check": "consent_denial_rate",
                "severity": "significant",
                "denial_rate_pct": round(denial_rate * 100, 1),
                "description": f"Consent denial rate {denial_rate:.1%} exceeds 10% — possible consent gate misconfiguration",
            })

    FINDINGS_OUT.write_text(json.dumps({
        "period": {"start": period_start, "end": period_end},
        "total_checks": 4,
        "findings": findings,
    }, indent=2))
    return findings

findings = run_automated_mock_audit("2026-01-01", "2026-03-31")
print(f"Automated mock audit complete: {len(findings)} finding(s)")
for f in findings:
    print(f"  [{f['severity'].upper()}] {f['check']}: {f['description'][:80]}")
Integrating Mock Audits into the Annual Compliance Calendar
A single pre-audit mock is better than none, but a quarterly lightweight mock surfaces issues while there is still time to build a full audit period of clean evidence:
Evidence completeness for Q4 of prior year
Blocker/significant findings list; remediation by Q2
Preparation for H1 SOC 2 period review
All findings remediated before real audit window
MRM controls — validation currency, exception register
Exception register current; overdue validations escalated
All vendor assessments current; incident runbook updated
Quarterly mock audits also serve a training function: control owners who prepare for a mock audit four times a year develop a fluency with their evidence that shows clearly in real audit interviews.
A finding that appears in two consecutive mock audits is a systemic issue, not an isolated gap. Track repeat findings separately:

Mock audits for Claude control programmes require a structured finding lifecycle — classify, assign, remediate, verify, close — backed by a programmatic finding register that tracks severity, ownership, due dates, and re-open history. The most common first-time blocker findings relate to logging completeness and alert-to-ticket gaps, not control design. Independent verification of each finding closure prevents owners from marking gaps as resolved before the evidence is actually in place. The close-out report delivered to the audit committee closes the loop and prepares the organisation for the real audit with documented confidence.
